title: Text, Immediate-Mode UI & a Match-3 Puzzle
html:
Why immediate-mode UI?

Every game needs menus, buttons, labels and HUDs. Two philosophies exist. Retained-mode UI keeps a tree of widget objects that you mutate; immediate-mode UI (IMGUI) keeps no widgets at all — each frame you simply call a function that draws the control and returns whether it was clicked. raylib's drawing model is already immediate, so IMGUI fits perfectly and is tiny to write.

The trick to IMGUI is that the function both draws and tests input in one pass. There is no retained state to keep in sync with your game state — the UI is a pure function of your data each frame.

ui.h
// A button: draws itself, returns true on the frame it is clicked #include inline bool UiButton(Rectangle r, const char* label) { Vector2 m = GetMousePosition(); bool hover = CheckCollisionPointRec(m, r); bool down = hover && IsMouseButtonDown(MOUSE_LEFT_BUTTON); bool click = hover && IsMouseButtonReleased(MOUSE_LEFT_BUTTON); Color fill = down ? DARKGRAY : (hover ? GRAY : LIGHTGRAY); DrawRectangleRec(r, fill); DrawRectangleLinesEx(r, 2, BLACK); // center the text in the button int fs = 20; int tw = MeasureText(label, fs); DrawText(label, (int)(r.x + (r.width - tw) / 2), (int)(r.y + (r.height - fs) / 2), fs, BLACK); return click; }

That is a complete, reusable button. Usage is gloriously direct: if (UiButton({20,20,160,48}, "Play")) StartGame();. No registration, no callbacks, no leaks.

Text layout

MeasureText gives pixel width so you can right-align, center, or build a vertical stack. A common helper is a cursor that advances as you add rows.

layout.h
// Stack panel: each call returns the next row rectangle struct Stack { float x, y, w, h, gap; }; inline Rectangle Row(Stack& s) { Rectangle r = { s.x, s.y, s.w, s.h }; s.y += s.h + s.gap; // advance cursor downward return r; } // usage Stack menu = { 40, 40, 200, 50, 12 }; if (UiButton(Row(menu), "New Game")) { /* ... */ } if (UiButton(Row(menu), "Continue")) { /* ... */ } if (UiButton(Row(menu), "Quit")) CloseWindow();
Tip.
Draw all UI after the world, and read input once per frame. Because IMGUI re-derives hover/click every frame, you never get stale state — a window resize or layout change just works on the next frame.

The Match-3 board

A match-3 is a 2D grid of gem ids. Core operations: swap two adjacent cells, find runs of three or more equal ids in a row or column, clear them, then collapse (gravity pulls gems down) and refill from the top. Repeating clear/collapse/refill produces cascades.

board.cpp
static const int W = 8, H = 8; int grid[H][W]; // 0..NUM_TYPES-1, or -1 for empty bool FindMatches(bool mark[H][W]) { bool any = false; for (int y=0; y// horizontal runs for (int y=0; y=0 && g==grid[y][x+1] && g==grid[y][x+2]) { mark[y][x]=mark[y][x+1]=mark[y][x+2]=true; any=true; } } // vertical runs for (int x=0; x=0 && g==grid[y+1][x] && g==grid[y+2][x]) { mark[y][x]=mark[y+1][x]=mark[y+2][x]=true; any=true; } } return any; } void Collapse() { for (int x=0; x// fill from bottom up for (int y=H-1; y>=0; y--) if (grid[y][x] >= 0) grid[write--][x] = grid[y][x]; for (int y=write; y>=0; y--) grid[y][x] = GetRandomValue(0, NUM_TYPES-1); // refill } }

Swap detection: tentatively swap, run FindMatches; if nothing matched, swap back (the classic "no, that's not a valid move" snap-back). A resolve loop then clears and cascades until FindMatches returns false, awarding more points per cascade tier.

resolve.cpp
void TrySwap(int ax,int ay,int bx,int by) { std::swap(grid[ay][ax], grid[by][bx]); bool mark[H][W]; if (!FindMatches(mark)) { std::swap(grid[ay][ax], grid[by][bx]); return; } int cascade = 0; do { for (int y=0;y
Lab.
Add a swapAnim timer so swaps lerp visually instead of snapping. Map mouse drag direction (dominant axis of the drag vector) to choose which neighbour to swap with.
🎮 Build This: Match-3 Puzzle — Gemfall

This starts a new genre: the match-3 puzzle. It is the first user of our reusable immediate-mode UI toolkit, which every later game's menus and HUD will reuse.

  1. Write ui.h with UiButton, a UiLabel, and a UiPanel (filled rounded rect) plus the Stack/Row layout helper.
  2. Build an 8×8 grid of gem ids; render each cell as a colored rounded rect sized to a fixed cell pitch.
  3. Click-select a gem, then click an adjacent gem to swap; implement snap-back when the swap makes no match.
  4. Implement FindMatches, clearing, Collapse, and refill; loop until stable to produce cascades, scaling points by cascade tier.
  5. Add a title screen and a game-over panel built entirely from your IMGUI toolkit, plus a live score label and a move-limit counter.

title: A Physics Puzzle: Impulses, Constraints & Stacking
html:
From integrator to physics engine

Back in the platformer (p4-9) you wrote semi-implicit Euler: v += a·dt; x += v·dt. A physics puzzle needs that to actually respond to collisions — bodies must bounce, slow down, and stack without sinking through each other. The pieces are impulses (instantaneous changes in velocity), restitution (bounciness), friction (tangential damping), and positional correction (pushing overlapping bodies apart).

Rigid body

For a 2D arcade puzzle, circles and axis-aligned boxes are enough. Store inverse mass (0 means immovable / infinite mass), which makes the math branch-free.

body.h
struct Body { Vector2 pos, vel; float radius; // circle bodies for simplicity float invMass; // 1/mass; 0 = static float restitution; // 0 = no bounce, 1 = perfect bounce float friction; // 0..1 tangential damping per contact };

Detecting and resolving a contact

When two circles overlap, the normal points from A to B. We resolve velocity along that normal with an impulse, then correct the position so they no longer overlap.

collide.cpp
#include "raymath.h" void ResolveCircles(Body& a, Body& b) { Vector2 d = Vector2Subtract(b.pos, a.pos); float dist = Vector2Length(d); float pen = a.radius + b.radius - dist; if (pen <= 0.0f) return; // not touching Vector2 n = (dist > 0.0001f) ? Vector2Scale(d, 1.0f/dist) : Vector2{1,0}; float invSum = a.invMass + b.invMass; if (invSum == 0.0f) return; // both static // 1) impulse along the normal (restitution) Vector2 rv = Vector2Subtract(b.vel, a.vel); float vn = Vector2DotProduct(rv, n); if (vn < 0) { // only if approaching float e = fminf(a.restitution, b.restitution); float j = -(1 + e) * vn / invSum; Vector2 imp = Vector2Scale(n, j); a.vel = Vector2Subtract(a.vel, Vector2Scale(imp, a.invMass)); b.vel = Vector2Add(b.vel, Vector2Scale(imp, b.invMass)); // 2) friction impulse along the tangent Vector2 t = Vector2Subtract(rv, Vector2Scale(n, vn)); if (Vector2Length(t) > 0.0001f) { t = Vector2Normalize(t); float jt = -Vector2DotProduct(rv, t) / invSum; float mu = sqrtf(a.friction * b.friction); jt = Clamp(jt, -j*mu, j*mu); // Coulomb cone Vector2 fimp = Vector2Scale(t, jt); a.vel = Vector2Subtract(a.vel, Vector2Scale(fimp, a.invMass)); b.vel = Vector2Add(b.vel, Vector2Scale(fimp, b.invMass)); } } // 3) positional correction (Baumgarte) to stop sinking const float slop = 0.01f, percent = 0.8f; float corr = fmaxf(pen - slop, 0.0f) / invSum * percent; Vector2 c = Vector2Scale(n, corr); a.pos = Vector2Subtract(a.pos, Vector2Scale(c, a.invMass)); b.pos = Vector2Add(b.pos, Vector2Scale(c, b.invMass)); }
Law.
Resolve velocity and position. Velocity impulses alone leave bodies overlapping; without the positional correction step, stacks slowly sink and jitter. The slop avoids fighting over sub-pixel overlaps.

The step loop and stacking

Integrate forces, then run several resolution iterations over all contact pairs. More iterations = stiffer stacks. This is a sequential-impulse solver — simple, stable enough for puzzles.

world.cpp
std::vector bodies; const Vector2 GRAVITY = { 0, 980 }; void Step(float dt) { for (auto& b : bodies) if (b.invMass > 0) b.vel = Vector2Add(b.vel, Vector2Scale(GRAVITY, dt)); for (int it = 0; it < 8; it++) // solver iterations for (size_t i=0;i

The slingshot is just an impulse you apply on release: capture the drag vector from the projectile, and on mouse-up set vel = (start - release) * power. The projectile is a dynamic body; the tower blocks are dynamic boxes; the ground and walls are static bodies with invMass = 0.

Tip.
Run physics on your fixed timestep from p4-2, not on raw frame time. A constant dt keeps restitution and stacking deterministic; variable dt makes bounces feel random and can blow up the solver.
🎮 Build This: Physics Puzzle — Slingshot Siege

This is a physics-puzzle genre game that extends the integrator you wrote for the platformer (p4-9), upgrading it into a small impulse-based rigid-body solver.

  1. Add restitution and friction fields to your body struct; implement ResolveCircles with impulse, friction cone, and positional correction.
  2. Run gravity integration plus 8 solver iterations on a fixed timestep each frame.
  3. Build a level: static ground/walls, a stack of dynamic blocks, and one or more target bodies.
  4. Implement a drag-to-aim slingshot that launches a projectile by applying a release impulse.
  5. Win when all targets are knocked off their platform (velocity-or-position check); reuse the IMGUI toolkit from p4-17 for shots-left, score, and a restart button.

title: AI, Dialogue, Save Files & an RPG
html:
What makes an RPG an RPG

Four systems define the genre: stats (numbers that grow), inventory (items you carry and use), dialogue (branching conversation trees), and turn-based combat (alternating actions resolved by those stats). On top sits persistence — save and load so progress survives. We will reuse the tilemap world (p4-10) and scene stack (p4-8) for exploration; this lesson adds the systems that turn a walkable map into an RPG.

Stats and characters

actor.h
struct Stats { int hp, maxHp, atk, def, level, xp; }; struct Actor { std::string name; Stats s; bool IsAlive() const { return s.hp > 0; } }; inline int DamageOf(const Actor& att, const Actor& def) { int raw = att.s.atk - def.s.def / 2; return raw < 1 ? 1 : raw; // always chip at least 1 } inline void GainXp(Actor& a, int xp) { a.s.xp += xp; while (a.s.xp >= a.s.level * 100) { // simple level curve a.s.xp -= a.s.level * 100; a.s.level++; a.s.maxHp += 10; a.s.hp = a.s.maxHp; a.s.atk += 2; a.s.def += 1; } }

Branching dialogue as data

Hard-coding conversations is misery. Model dialogue as a graph of nodes: each node has text plus a list of choices, and each choice points to the next node id (or -1 to end). This is data-driven — you can later load it from a file.

dialogue.h
struct Choice { std::string text; int next; }; struct Node { std::string speaker, line; std::vector choices; }; struct Dialogue { std::vector nodes; int current = 0; bool done() const { return current < 0; } void choose(int i) { current = nodes[current].choices[i].next; } }; // build one in code (or parse from a file) Dialogue MakeIntro() { Dialogue d; d.nodes.push_back({ "Elder", "The mines are cursed. Will you help?", { {"I will.", 1}, {"Not my problem.", 2} } }); d.nodes.push_back({ "Elder", "Bless you. Take this potion.", { {"...", -1} } }); d.nodes.push_back({ "Elder", "Coward.", { {"...", -1} } }); return d; }

Rendering uses the IMGUI toolkit from p4-17: draw the current node's line in a panel, then one UiButton per choice. Clicking calls choose; when done(), pop the dialogue scene.

Turn-based combat

Combat is a tiny state machine: it is the player's turn or the enemy's, and you resolve one action then hand over. Reusing the FSM idea (formalized next lesson) keeps it readable.

battle.cpp
enum class Turn { Player, Enemy, Won, Lost }; Turn turn = Turn::Player; void PlayerAttack(Actor& hero, Actor& foe) { if (turn != Turn::Player) return; foe.s.hp -= DamageOf(hero, foe); if (!foe.IsAlive()) { GainXp(hero, 50); turn = Turn::Won; return; } turn = Turn::Enemy; } void EnemyAct(Actor& hero, Actor& foe) { // trivial AI if (turn != Turn::Enemy) return; hero.s.hp -= DamageOf(foe, hero); turn = hero.IsAlive() ? Turn::Player : Turn::Lost; }
Tip.
Even a one-line enemy AI feels better with a small decision: if foe.hp < maxHp/4 and it has a heal item, heal instead of attacking. That single branch reads as "the enemy is desperate" to the player.

Saving and loading

For a save file, serialize your game state to a simple key/value text format with std::ofstream and read it back with std::ifstream. Text is debuggable and portable; binary is faster but unforgiving. Use raylib's GetApplicationDirectory() or a known path to place the file.

save.cpp
#include #include void Save(const Actor& h, int mapX, int mapY, const char* path) { std::ofstream f(path); f << "name " << h.name << "\n"; f << "hp " << h.s.hp << " " << h.s.maxHp << "\n"; f << "stats " << h.s.atk << " " << h.s.def << " " << h.s.level << " " << h.s.xp << "\n"; f << "pos " << mapX << " " << mapY << "\n"; } bool Load(Actor& h, int& mapX, int& mapY, const char* path) { std::ifstream f(path); if (!f) return false; std::string key; while (f >> key) { if (key=="name") f >> h.name; else if (key=="hp") f >> h.s.hp >> h.s.maxHp; else if (key=="stats") f >> h.s.atk >> h.s.def >> h.s.level >> h.s.xp; else if (key=="pos") f >> mapX >> mapY; } return true; }
Danger.
Never trust a save file. A corrupt or hand-edited file can set maxHp to garbage. Validate ranges on load (hp = Clamp(hp,0,maxHp)) so a bad file degrades gracefully instead of crashing.
🎮 Build This: RPG — Emberhold

This starts a new genre: the RPG. It reuses your tilemap + 2D camera (p4-10), the scene stack (p4-8), and the IMGUI toolkit (p4-17), and adds stats, inventory, dialogue, combat, and save files.

  1. Walk a tilemap town with the player as an Actor; place NPCs that, on interaction, push a dialogue scene.
  2. Implement the dialogue graph and render it with IMGUI choice buttons.
  3. Add an inventory (vector<Item>) with a usable healing potion that modifies stats.
  4. Trigger turn-based battles on enemy contact; resolve with the DamageOf/GainXp math and a small enemy AI.
  5. Add Save/Load to a text file, validating all loaded values; expose Save, Load, and Continue on the title menu.

title: Game AI: FSM, A* Pathfinding, Steering & Tower Defense
html:
Three pillars of game AI

Most game AI is built from three reusable tools. A finite state machine decides what to do. A* pathfinding figures out how to get there through a grid. Steering behaviors turn that path into smooth, frame-rate-independent movement. A tower defense exercises all three: enemies path from spawn to goal, towers run FSMs (idle → acquire → fire → cooldown), and projectiles or enemies steer.

A reusable FSM

The lightest FSM is an enum plus a switch. Keep the transition logic in one place so behavior is easy to read.

tower.cpp
enum class TState { Idle, Firing, Cooldown }; struct Tower { Vector2 pos; float range, fireRate, timer = 0; TState st = TState::Idle; }; void TickTower(Tower& t, std::vector& foes, float dt) { t.timer -= dt; Enemy* target = NearestInRange(t, foes); switch (t.st) { case TState::Idle: if (target) t.st = TState::Firing; break; case TState::Firing: if (!target) { t.st = TState::Idle; break; } if (t.timer <= 0) { Shoot(t, *target); t.timer = t.fireRate; t.st = TState::Cooldown; } break; case TState::Cooldown: if (t.timer <= 0) t.st = target ? TState::Firing : TState::Idle; break; } }

A* pathfinding on a grid

A* finds the shortest path by expanding the node with the lowest f = g + h, where g is cost-so-far and h is a heuristic estimate to the goal. On a 4-connected grid, Manhattan distance is an admissible heuristic.

astar.cpp
#include #include struct P { int x, y; }; static int H(P a, P b) { return abs(a.x-b.x) + abs(a.y-b.y); } // Manhattan static int Key(P p) { return p.y * MAP_W + p.x; } std::vector

AStar(P start, P goal) { struct Open { int f; P p; bool operator<(const Open& o) const { return f > o.f; } }; std::priority_queue open; // min-heap via reversed less std::unordered_map g, came; open.push({ H(start,goal), start }); g[Key(start)] = 0; const int dx[4]={1,-1,0,0}, dy[4]={0,0,1,-1}; while (!open.empty()) { P c = open.top().p; open.pop(); if (c.x==goal.x && c.y==goal.y) break; for (int i=0;i<4;i++) { P n = { c.x+dx[i], c.y+dy[i] }; if (!Walkable(n)) continue; int ng = g[Key(c)] + 1; auto it = g.find(Key(n)); if (it==g.end() || ng < it->second) { g[Key(n)] = ng; came[Key(n)] = Key(c); open.push({ ng + H(n,goal), n }); } } } // reconstruct path from came-from map std::vector

path; int k = Key(goal); while (came.count(k)) { path.push_back({ k%MAP_W, k/MAP_W }); k = came[k]; } std::reverse(path.begin(), path.end()); return path; }

Law.
The heuristic must never overestimate the true cost, or A* can return a non-optimal path. Manhattan distance on a 4-grid is admissible; if you allow diagonals, switch to octile/Chebyshev distance.

Steering along the path

A* gives waypoints; seek steering moves the enemy toward the current waypoint and advances when close. Steering keeps motion continuous regardless of framerate because it works in velocity space.

steer.cpp
#include "raymath.h" Vector2 Seek(Vector2 pos, Vector2 vel, Vector2 target, float maxSpeed) { Vector2 desired = Vector2Scale( Vector2Normalize(Vector2Subtract(target, pos)), maxSpeed); return Vector2Subtract(desired, vel); // steering force } void TickEnemy(Enemy& e, float dt) { if (e.wp >= (int)e.path.size()) return; // reached goal Vector2 tgt = TileCenter(e.path[e.wp]); Vector2 force = Seek(e.pos, e.vel, tgt, e.speed); e.vel = Vector2Add(e.vel, Vector2Scale(force, dt * 6.0f)); e.vel = Vector2ClampValue(e.vel, 0, e.speed); e.pos = Vector2Add(e.pos, Vector2Scale(e.vel, dt)); if (Vector2Distance(e.pos, tgt) < 4.0f) e.wp++; // next waypoint }
Tip.
Recompute paths only when the map changes (a tower placed on the route), not every frame — A* is the most expensive call in the game. Cache the path per enemy and only re-run on a "map dirty" flag.

Waves

A wave is a timed spawn schedule: a list of (enemy type, count, interval). A wave manager counts down between spawns, then waits for the field to clear before starting the next wave and raising difficulty.

🎮 Build This: Tower Defense — Gridline Defense

This starts a new genre: strategy / tower defense. It reuses the tilemap (p4-10) for the map grid and the IMGUI toolkit (p4-17) for the build bar.

  1. Build a grid map with a spawn tile and a goal tile; mark non-buildable path tiles.
  2. Implement A* and have each enemy path from spawn to goal, following waypoints with seek steering.
  3. Let the player place towers on buildable tiles (deduct gold); set the "map dirty" flag so enemies re-path around new blockers.
  4. Give towers an FSM (Idle/Firing/Cooldown) that acquires the nearest in-range enemy and fires projectiles.
  5. Add a wave manager with escalating counts, a lives counter that drops when enemies reach the goal, and gold rewards per kill.

title: Data-Driven Design: An Endless Runner & a Rhythm Game
html:
Why data-driven?

So far levels lived in code. Data-driven design moves content — level layouts, tuning constants, beat maps — into external files the program loads at runtime. The win is iteration speed: a designer (or you) can retune a jump arc or rewrite a chart without recompiling. Two genres make this concrete: an endless runner driven by chunk and config data, and a rhythm game driven by a beat map synced to audio.

A tiny config/loader

You do not need a JSON library. A line-oriented key value format parses in a few lines and is plenty for configs and chunks.

config.cpp
#include #include #include std::unordered_map LoadConfig(const char* path) { std::unordered_map cfg; std::ifstream f(path); std::string key; float val; while (f >> key >> val) cfg[key] = val; // "gravity 2200" return cfg; } // usage auto cfg = LoadConfig("data/runner.cfg"); float gravity = cfg["gravity"]; float jumpVel = cfg["jump_vel"]; float baseSpeed = cfg["scroll_speed"];

The endless runner

An endless runner scrolls the world left while the player holds a lane and jumps obstacles. "Endless" means you stream chunks: pre-authored slices of obstacles loaded from a file and recycled as they leave the screen.

runner.cpp
// chunk file: each line is "offsetX gapY" for one obstacle struct Obstacle { float x; Rectangle box; }; std::vector> chunks; // loaded from files float scroll = 0, speed; std::vector active; void Spawn(float atX) { auto& c = chunks[GetRandomValue(0, (int)chunks.size()-1)]; for (auto o : c) { o.x += atX; active.push_back(o); } } void UpdateRunner(float dt) { speed += dt * 5.0f; // ramp difficulty for (auto& o : active) o.x -= speed * dt; // recycle: drop off-screen, append new chunk on the right while (!active.empty() && active.front().x < -64) active.erase(active.begin()); if (active.empty() || active.back().x < GetScreenWidth()) Spawn(GetScreenWidth() + 200); }
Tip.
Author chunks by difficulty band and only draw from bands at or below the current speed. That gives a smooth ramp without hand-placing an infinite level, and reuses the same loader for every chunk.

Rhythm timing: the hard part is audio

Rhythm games live or die on timing. The golden rule: judge hits against the audio playback position, never against accumulated frame time. Audio and the CPU clock drift; the song is the source of truth. raylib exposes GetMusicTimePlayed for exactly this.

beatmap.cpp
// beat map file: each line is "timeSec lane" struct Note { float time; int lane; bool hit=false; }; std::vector notes; Note LoadNotes(std::ifstream& f) { /* read time+lane per line */ } Music song; float approach = 1.2f; // seconds a note is visible before its hit time void DrawNotes() { float now = GetMusicTimePlayed(song); // audio clock = truth for (auto& n : notes) { if (n.hit) continue; float dt = n.time - now; // time until it should be hit if (dt < -0.2f) n.hit = true; // missed window else if (dt < approach) { float y = Lerp(-40, HIT_LINE_Y, 1 - dt/approach); DrawCircle(LaneX(n.lane), (int)y, 18, SKYBLUE); } } }
judge.cpp
const char* Judge(int lane) { float now = GetMusicTimePlayed(song); Note* best = nullptr; float bestErr = 0.15f; // 150ms window for (auto& n : notes) { if (n.hit || n.lane != lane) continue; float err = fabsf(n.time - now); if (err < bestErr) { bestErr = err; best = &n; } } if (!best) return "MISS"; best->hit = true; return bestErr < 0.04f ? "PERFECT" : (bestErr < 0.09f ? "GREAT" : "GOOD"); }
Law.
Output audio has latency. Measure your device's offset once (a calibration screen) and subtract it from GetMusicTimePlayed before judging. Without calibration, every player on slower hardware will feel "early."
Lab.
Write a tiny tool mode that records timestamps when you tap a key over the playing song, then dumps them as a beat-map file. You just built a chart editor — data-driven design feeding itself.
🎮 Build This: Endless Runner + Rhythm — Pulse Run

This starts two new genres: the endless runner and the rhythm game, bound together by data-driven loading. Reuse the fixed timestep (p4-2), the physics jump (p4-9), and the IMGUI toolkit (p4-17).

  1. Write the line-based config and file loaders; load gravity, jump, and scroll speed from a .cfg file.
  2. Build the runner: a jumping player, chunk files streamed and recycled, AABB collision for game-over, distance score.
  3. Build a separate rhythm scene: load a beat map of (time, lane) notes and a music track.
  4. Spawn notes by approach time and judge taps against GetMusicTimePlayed with PERFECT/GREAT/GOOD/MISS windows.
  5. Add an audio-offset calibration screen, then sync the runner's obstacle spawns to the beat map so the two genres become one beat-driven game.

title: A Fighting Game: Hitboxes, Frame Data & Combos
html:
The genre of precision

Fighting games are the most timing-exact 2D genre. Everything is measured in frames at a fixed 60 Hz: an attack has startup, active, and recovery frames; a move connects when an active hitbox overlaps an opponent's hurtbox. Layer on input buffering (so a slightly-early press still registers), special-move motions (quarter-circle, etc.), and a round system, and you have the genre. This is where the fixed timestep from p4-2 is non-negotiable.

Frame data

Each move is pure data: how long until it hurts, how long it hurts, and how long you are stuck afterward. Storing it as data lets you tune balance without touching logic.

move.h
struct Move { int startup, active, recovery; // in frames @ 60fps int damage, hitstun, blockstun; Rectangle hitbox; // offset from fighter origin, active phase only }; const Move JAB = { 3, 2, 7, 4, 12, 6, { 40, -20, 36, 18 } }; const Move HEAVY = { 9, 4, 18, 12, 20, 10, { 44, -24, 48, 24 } };

The fighter state

fighter.h
enum class FState { Idle, Walk, Attacking, Hitstun, Blockstun, KO }; struct Fighter { Vector2 pos; int facing = 1; int hp = 100; FState st = FState::Idle; const Move* move = nullptr; int frame = 0; // frames elapsed in current action Rectangle hurtbox; // body, relative to pos }; inline bool InActive(const Fighter& f) { return f.st == FState::Attacking && f.move && f.frame >= f.move->startup && f.frame < f.move->startup + f.move->active; }

Hitbox vs hurtbox

Each fixed step, advance frame counters, then if an attacker is in its active window, test its (facing-flipped) hitbox against the defender's hurtbox.

combat.cpp
Rectangle WorldBox(const Fighter& f, Rectangle local) { float x = (f.facing > 0) ? f.pos.x + local.x : f.pos.x - local.x - local.width; // mirror return { x, f.pos.y + local.y, local.width, local.height }; } void ResolveHits(Fighter& att, Fighter& def, bool defBlocking) { if (!InActive(att)) return; Rectangle hb = WorldBox(att, att.move->hitbox); Rectangle hu = WorldBox(def, def.hurtbox); if (!CheckCollisionRecs(hb, hu)) return; if (defBlocking) { def.st = FState::Blockstun; def.frame = 0; def.hp -= att.move->damage / 4; // chip damage def.stunFrames = att.move->blockstun; } else { def.st = FState::Hitstun; def.frame = 0; def.hp -= att.move->damage; def.stunFrames = att.move->hitstun; // combo window! } att.move = nullptr; // one hit per active window }
Law.
Hit detection must run on the fixed step, and each active window may only connect once. Clear the move (or set a "has hit" flag) on contact, or one swing will deal damage every frame it overlaps.

Input buffering and motions

Humans press buttons a few frames early. A buffer stores recent inputs so an action that becomes legal within a few frames still fires. The same ring buffer detects motion inputs like quarter-circle-forward (down, down-forward, forward + punch).

input.cpp
struct Cmd { int dir; bool punch; int age; }; // dir: numpad notation 1..9 std::deque buffer; void PushInput(int dir, bool punch) { for (auto& c : buffer) c.age++; buffer.push_front({ dir, punch, 0 }); while (buffer.size() > 20) buffer.pop_back(); // ~20 frame history } bool QuarterCircleForward(int facing) { // look for 2 -> 3 -> 6 + punch within the last few frames int fwd = (facing > 0) ? 6 : 4; int dfwd = (facing > 0) ? 3 : 1; bool punch=false, sawF=false, sawDF=false, sawD=false; for (auto& c : buffer) { if (c.age > 12) break; // motion must be recent if (c.punch && c.dir==fwd) punch=true; if (c.dir==fwd) sawF=true; if (c.dir==dfwd) sawDF=true; if (c.dir==2) sawD=true; } return punch && sawF && sawDF && sawD; }
Tip.
A combo exists purely because hitstun outlasts your move's recovery: if you can act before the opponent recovers, your next move connects. Tune the difference between your fastest move's recovery and the heavy's hitstun to decide which links are possible.
Lab.
Add a debug overlay that draws hitboxes in red and hurtboxes in blue, with the current frame number. You cannot balance frame data you cannot see.
🎮 Build This: Fighting Game — Frame Fighters

This starts a new genre: the 2D fighting game. It depends on the fixed-timestep loop (p4-2) for deterministic frames, the FSM idea (p4-20) for fighter states, and the IMGUI toolkit (p4-17) for health bars and round UI.

  1. Define moves as frame data and give each fighter an FSM with startup/active/recovery phases driven on the fixed step.
  2. Implement facing-aware hitbox/hurtbox overlap with one-hit-per-active-window and hit/block stun.
  3. Add a ring-buffer input system with buffering and a quarter-circle-forward special-move detector.
  4. Make combos possible by tuning hitstun vs recovery; add a damage-and-stun loop so links chain.
  5. Wrap it in a best-of-three round system with a timer, round-win pips, and a rematch button, plus a debug box overlay.

title: Procedural Generation: A Roguelike & a Simulation Sandbox
html:
Letting the computer build the world

Procedural generation creates content from rules and randomness instead of by hand. Two classic uses anchor this lesson: a roguelike dungeon built by carving rooms and corridors, and a simulation sandbox grown with cellular automata. Both hinge on one discipline: a seeded RNG, so the same seed always reproduces the same world — essential for sharing runs and for debugging.

Seeded randomness

Use a deterministic generator you control, not a global one. C++'s <random> gives reproducible streams.

rng.h
#include struct Rng { std::mt19937 gen; explicit Rng(uint32_t seed) : gen(seed) {} int range(int lo, int hi) { // inclusive std::uniform_int_distribution d(lo, hi); return d(gen); } bool chance(float p) { std::uniform_real_distribution d(0,1); return d(gen) < p; } };
Law.
One seed, one world. Never mix in rand(), the system clock, or unordered-container iteration order during generation — any nondeterminism breaks reproducibility and makes "seed 1234" useless to share.

Roguelike: rooms and corridors

The simplest readable dungeon generator: scatter non-overlapping rooms, then connect each room's center to the previous one with L-shaped corridors carved into a wall-filled grid.

dungeon.cpp
enum Tile { WALL, FLOOR }; int map[H][W]; struct Room { int x,y,w,h; int cx() const {return x+w/2;} int cy() const {return y+h/2;} }; void Carve(int x,int y,int w,int h){ for(int j=y;j Generate(Rng& r) { for(int j=0;j rooms; for (int tries=0; tries<60 && rooms.size()<12; tries++) { Room rm{ r.range(1,W-10), r.range(1,H-8), r.range(4,8), r.range(4,6) }; bool overlap=false; for (auto& o : rooms) if (rm.x < o.x+o.w+1 && rm.x+rm.w+1 > o.x && rm.y < o.y+o.h+1 && rm.y+rm.h+1 > o.y) { overlap=true; break; } if (overlap) continue; Carve(rm.x, rm.y, rm.w, rm.h); if (!rooms.empty()) { // L-corridor to previous Room& p = rooms.back(); HCorr(p.cx(), rm.cx(), p.cy()); VCorr(p.cy(), rm.cy(), rm.cx()); } rooms.push_back(rm); } return rooms; }

Turn-based movement is the genre's signature: the world only advances when the player acts. Read a move key, attempt the step (attack if a monster is there, walk if floor is free), and only then tick every monster once.

turn.cpp
void TryPlayerTurn() { int dx=0, dy=0; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_RIGHT)) dx=1; else if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_LEFT)) dx=-1; else if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_DOWN)) dy=1; else if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_UP)) dy=-1; else return; // no input = world frozen int nx=player.x+dx, ny=player.y+dy; if (Monster* m = MonsterAt(nx,ny)) Attack(player, *m); else if (map[ny][nx]==FLOOR) { player.x=nx; player.y=ny; } for (auto& m : monsters) MonsterTurn(m); // one tick each, after the player }

Simulation: cellular automata

The sandbox grows organic cave-like layouts (or models gliders, fire, water) by repeatedly applying a neighbor rule. Start with random noise, then "smooth": a cell becomes wall if most neighbors are walls. A few passes yield natural caverns.

cave.cpp
void SeedNoise(Rng& r, float fill) { for(int j=0;j// one CA pass into a copy, then swap static int next[H][W]; for(int j=1;j 4) ? WALL : (n < 4 ? FLOOR : map[j][i]); } for(int j=1;j
Tip.
Always write a CA pass into a separate buffer and swap; updating in place lets a cell "see" neighbors you already changed this pass, corrupting the rule. Run 4-5 passes for caves; add a flood-fill to discard tiny disconnected pockets.
Lab.
Show the seed on screen and let the player type one in. Run the generator twice with the same seed and confirm pixel-identical maps — your proof that generation is deterministic.
🎮 Build This: Roguelike + Simulation — Caverns & Cells

This starts two new genres: the turn-based roguelike and the grid simulation sandbox, sharing one seeded RNG and tile grid. Reuse the tilemap renderer (p4-10) and the IMGUI toolkit (p4-17).

  1. Build the seeded Rng and route all generation randomness through it.
  2. Generate a rooms-and-corridors dungeon; place the player, stairs, and monsters from seeded rolls.
  3. Implement turn-based movement where the world ticks only after the player acts, with bump-to-attack combat and field-of-view.
  4. Add a sandbox mode: seed noise and run cellular-automata smoothing passes to grow caves; let the player paint/erase cells and watch a Life-style rule evolve.
  5. Display and accept a seed string; verify identical output for identical seeds, and add a "descend" button to regenerate the next floor.

title: Memory, Performance, Game-Feel, Shipping & the Capstone
html:
From working to shipped

You can build any genre now. This final lesson is about making a game fast, juicy, and distributable. We cover memory and performance discipline, shader and game-feel polish, and cross-platform packaging — then assemble a capstone that pulls multiple genres from this track into one binary.

Memory: own it deliberately

Two habits matter most. First, pool frequently created/destroyed objects (bullets, particles, enemies) instead of allocating each one — you already pooled bullets in the twin-stick shooter (p4-13). Second, prefer contiguous std::vector over node-based containers so the CPU cache stays warm. RAII (p4-8) ensures every resource frees itself.

pool.h
template struct Pool { std::vector items; std::vector freeList; int spawn() { // O(1), no allocation if (!freeList.empty()) { int i=freeList.back(); freeList.pop_back(); return i; } items.push_back(T{}); return (int)items.size()-1; } void kill(int i) { items[i] = T{}; freeList.push_back(i); } };

Performance: measure, do not guess

Optimize only what a profiler flags. A zero-cost first pass is timing your own sections with the high-resolution clock and watching GetFPS().

profile.h
#include struct Scope { const char* name; std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::time_point t0; Scope(const char* n): name(n), t0(std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now()) {} ~Scope() { auto us = std::chrono::duration_cast( std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now() - t0).count(); TraceLog(LOG_INFO, "%s: %lld us", name, (long long)us); } }; // usage: { Scope s("physics"); Step(dt); } // logs on scope exit
Law.
Profile before optimizing, and optimize the biggest cost first. Most frame time hides in one or two hot spots (often draw calls or O(n²) collision); shaving a function that costs 1% is wasted effort.

Game feel: juice

"Juice" is the cheap, high-impact polish that makes a game feel alive: screen shake, hit-stop, particles, tweened scale, and easing. None of it changes the rules — it changes the feedback.

juice.cpp
float shake = 0; void AddShake(float amt) { shake = fmaxf(shake, amt); } Camera2D ApplyShake(Camera2D cam, float dt) { shake = fmaxf(0, shake - dt * 12.0f); // decay cam.offset.x += GetRandomValue(-100,100)/100.0f * shake; cam.offset.y += GetRandomValue(-100,100)/100.0f * shake; return cam; } float hitStop = 0; // freeze frames on impact float ScaleDt(float dt) { if (hitStop>0){ hitStop-=dt; return dt*0.05f; } return dt; }

Shaders add the visual layer. raylib loads GLSL fragment shaders with LoadShader; a full-screen pass over a render texture gives bloom, CRT, vignette, or palette effects.

postfx.cpp
RenderTexture2D scene = LoadRenderTexture(W, H); Shader fx = LoadShader(0, "data/vignette.fs"); // each frame: BeginTextureMode(scene); ClearBackground(BLACK); DrawWorld(); EndTextureMode(); BeginDrawing(); BeginShaderMode(fx); // flip Y: render textures are upside-down DrawTextureRec(scene.texture, { 0,0,(float)W,-(float)H }, {0,0}, WHITE); EndShaderMode(); EndDrawing();
Tip.
Add juice last and gate it behind a settings toggle. Shake and hit-stop are wonderful in small doses; accessibility-minded players need to turn them off. Never let polish alter the simulation timestep.

Shipping a cross-platform binary

You built with CMake in p4-1, so packaging is mostly configuration. Build in Release, copy your data/ folder beside the executable, and produce per-OS bundles. raylib links statically by default, which keeps distribution simple.

build & package
# configure an optimized build cmake -S . -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release cmake --build build --config Release # bundle the binary with its assets mkdir -p dist/Capstone cp build/Capstone dist/Capstone/ # .exe on Windows, .app payload on macOS cp -r data dist/Capstone/ # zip dist/Capstone and ship it
Danger.
Ship assets with relative paths resolved from the executable's directory (use ChangeDirectory(GetApplicationDirectory()) at startup). A game that loads data/x.png from the current directory works when you run it from the project root and breaks the instant a player double-clicks it elsewhere.
Lab.
Add a CMake install() rule and a packaging target so cmake --build build --target package produces a zip per platform with assets included. Wire it into a CI matrix (Windows/macOS/Linux) to get reproducible release artifacts on every tag.
🎮 Build This: Capstone — Multi-Genre Showcase

This is a concept-and-capstone lesson: it does not start a new genre but unifies several you already built into one shippable binary, driven by the scene stack (p4-8) and IMGUI toolkit (p4-17).

  1. Build a hub menu (IMGUI) that launches at least three modes from this track — e.g. the raycast FPS (p4-14), the pseudo-3D racer (p4-15), and the roguelike (p4-23) — each pushed as a scene.
  2. Pool all transient objects, add a per-section profiler scope, and fix the single biggest hot spot it reveals.
  3. Add a full-screen shader post-pass plus screen shake and hit-stop, all behind a settings toggle.
  4. Make every asset load relative to the executable directory; verify the build runs from a fresh folder.
  5. Produce Release builds for Windows, macOS, and Linux via CMake, bundle data/, and zip each as a distributable artifact.
